summary: I was born and lived in Vietnam, where I served in the South Vietnamese army from 1957 until the fall of Saigon in 1975. Vietnam was the place where I fought and later suffered under the new communist regime. After the war ended, life in Vietnam became very difficult for me and my family, especially during my time in the reeducation camp and the years of surveillance that followed.

April 30, 1975, was a day I will never forget. That morning, I was commanding an armored squadron and fighting the enemy when I heard the order to stop fighting and go home because the war was over. It was a devastating moment for me, marking the end of the South Vietnamese government and the beginning of a very hard life under communist rule.

After the war, the Communist Party of Vietnam took control and sent many of us, including myself, to reeducation camps. They ran the government and enforced harsh policies to reshape our thinking through Marxism and Leninism. The Party praised leaders like Ho Chi Minh and demanded ideological reform to erase any reactionary ideas, which made life very difficult for former South Vietnamese soldiers like me.

Ho Chi Minh was the great leader praised by the Communist Party during my time in the reeducation camp. We were taught to sing his praises and follow the communist ideology he represented. Although I was a soldier fighting against the communists, his image was everywhere during those years of forced ideological reform.

One month after the war ended, I was sent to a government-run reeducation camp where I spent six years. We were told it was 'reeducation through labor,' but it was really a form of imprisonment without trial. The days were filled with hard labor and nights with lessons on Marxism and Leninism, all aimed at breaking our former beliefs and making us loyal to the communist regime.

During my imprisonment in the reeducation camp, we were taught the theories of Marxism as part of the ideological reform. These lessons were meant to change our thinking and make us accept the communist way of life. I learned about Marxism alongside Leninism, but it was all forced and part of the government's effort to control us.

Like Marxism, Leninism was taught to us in the reeducation camp to reshape our beliefs. The communist authorities used Leninism to justify their control and to push us to accept their ideology. These lessons were part of the daily routine, especially at night, and were meant to eliminate any opposition to the new regime.

The United States became my new home after I applied for refuge through their Orderly Departure Program in 1990. I arrived there with my wife and two youngest sons in 1994, and later was able to sponsor my older children and their families. The U.S. gave us freedom and a chance for a better life after years of hardship in Vietnam.

The Orderly Departure Program was a U.S. government initiative that allowed me to leave Vietnam legally and safely. I applied in 1990 because I met the criteria of having spent time in a reeducation camp. Thanks to ODP, I was able to bring my wife and youngest sons to the United States in 1994, which changed our lives dramatically.

Minnesota became our new home when we arrived in the United States in 1994. My wife, sons, and eventually my older children and their families all settled there. Minnesota gave us stability, jobs, and a place where our children could go to school and grow up in freedom.
